```{r step3a_summary_table}
       fmt_cell(h32_NA_perc_love_pos, "NA_sim_perc:cwe_ness"))
)

tbl_3a <- do.call(rbind, lapply(rows, function(r) {
  data.frame(
    Outcome    = r[[1]],
    Affect     = r[[2]],
    Similarity = r[[3]],
    Parameter  = r[[4]],
    Positive   = r[[6]],
    Negative   = r[[5]],
    check.names = FALSE,
    stringsAsFactors = FALSE
  )
}))

note_html <- paste0(
  "<em>Note.</em> ",
  "Coefficients with * p &lt; .05, ** p &lt; .01, ",
  "*** p &lt; .001. Significant coefficients (p &lt; .05) in ",
  "<strong>bold</strong>. Similarity: main effect of similarity at average ",
  "we-ness. We-ness: main effect of trait we-ness at average similarity. ",
  "Sim &times; We-ness: interaction term (H3.1 for PA, H3.2 for NA; ",
  "expected positive). All models include a dyad-level random intercept."
)

tab <- kable(tbl_3a,
  format    = "html",
  col.names = c("Outcome", "Affect", "Similarity", "Parameter",
                "Positive Interaction", "Negative Interaction"),
  align     = c("l", "l", "l", "l", "c", "c"),
  booktabs  = TRUE,
  escape    = FALSE
) %>%
  kable_styling(
    bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
    full_width = FALSE,
    position   = "left",
    font_size  = 12
  ) %>%
  add_header_above(c(" " = 4,
                      "Interaction Valence" = 2)) %>%
  row_spec(4, extra_css = "border-top: 1px solid #aaa !important;") %>%
  row_spec(7, extra_css = "border-top: 1px solid #aaa !important;") %>%
  row_spec(10, extra_css = "border-top: 1px solid #aaa !important;") %>%
  row_spec(13, extra_css = "border-top: 2px solid #bbb !important;") %>%
  row_spec(16, extra_css = "border-top: 1px solid #aaa !important;") %>%
  row_spec(19, extra_css = "border-top: 1px solid #aaa !important;") %>%
  row_spec(22, extra_css = "border-top: 1px solid #aaa !important;") %>%
  collapse_rows(columns = 1:3, valign = "middle")

tab_out <- paste0(
  '<style>
    table.table > thead > tr:last-child > th {
      border-bottom: 2px solid #bbb !important;
    }
  </style>',sub(
  "</table>",
  paste0(
    "<tfoot><tr><td colspan=\"6\" ",
    "style=\"border-top: 0; padding: 8px 0 0 0; font-size: 11px; ",
    "line-height: 1.35; text-align: left;\">",
    note_html,
    "</td></tr></tfoot></table>"
  ),
  as.character(tab),
  fixed = TRUE
)
)

attributes(tab_out) <- attributes(tab)
tab_out
```

Across all 16 Step 3a models, only one moderation by trait we-ness reaches significance: perceived NA similarity $\times$ we-ness predicting closeness in the negative interaction ($\beta_3 = 1.284$, $p = .005$). This crossover interaction reveals that perceiving shared negative affect during a disagreement predicts less closeness for low-we-ness couples but more closeness for high-we-ness couples. The love model in the same context shows a suggestive but non-significant pattern in the same direction ($\beta_3 = 0.758$, $p = .119$). No other interaction term approaches significance.

**H3.1 is not supported:** we-ness does not amplify the positive PA similarity effect for any outcome or context. If anything, the descriptive pattern (visible in the probing tables) trends in the opposite direction, with PA similarity slopes tending to be larger at low we-ness, but no interaction term is significant.

**H3.2 receives partial support**, but only for perceived (not actual) NA similarity, only for closeness (not love), and only in the negative interaction. The hypothesis assumed we-ness would buffer a negative similarity effect, but the mechanism is different: we-ness determines whether perceived shared NA is experienced as connecting or threatening, producing a crossover rather than a buffering pattern.

The most consistent finding across the Step 3a models is the **direct effect of we-ness on post-interaction evaluations**, particularly in the positive interaction context. We-ness significantly predicts higher love and closeness after a positive interaction across multiple model specifications (ranging from $8.3$ to $13.7$ points per unit of we-ness). In the negative interaction, significant we-ness direct effects emerge specifically in the NA models ($14.1$-$18.3$), where the similarity predictor absorbs less between-dyad variance, leaving more for we-ness to capture. These direct effects indicate that trait we-ness is a robust predictor of post-interaction evaluations independently of affect similarity.

# Step 3b: Context Moderation (H3.3–H3.4)

We examine whether the similarity–evaluation association differs between the positive and negative interaction contexts. As specified in the preregistration, we approach this in two complementary ways: first a descriptive comparison of stratified models, then a formal pooled test with context as a moderator.

## Descriptive Comparison (Stratified Models)

The Step 1a models already provide the stratified estimates for actual similarity: the similarity–evaluation coefficients were estimated separately within each interaction context. For perceived similarity, we estimate analogous models substituting perceived for actual similarity within each context, to enable the same descriptive comparison.

### PA Similarity

#### Actual PA Similarity

The Step 1a models (`h11_PA_love_neg`, `h11_PA_love_pos`, `h11_PA_close_neg`, `h11_PA_close_pos`) provide the stratified estimates. The coefficients are summarized below for convenience:

+----------------+-----------------------------+-----------------------------+
| Outcome        | Negative Interaction        | Positive Interaction        |
+================+=============================+=============================+
| Love           | $\beta = 0.379$, $p = .020$ | $\beta = 0.459$, $p = .007$ |
+----------------+-----------------------------+-----------------------------+
| Closeness      | $\beta = 0.432$, $p = .015$ | $\beta = 0.376$, $p = .016$ |
+----------------+-----------------------------+-----------------------------+

Both coefficients are significant in both contexts. For love, the slope is somewhat larger in the positive interaction ($0.459$ vs. $0.379$); for closeness, somewhat larger in the negative interaction ($0.432$ vs. $0.376$). The differences are small and not consistent in direction across outcomes. The formal test below will determine whether these differences are statistically reliable.

#### Perceived PA Similarity

```{r desc_PA_perc_love_neg}
#| output-fold: true
#| output-summary: "Descriptive: perceived PA → Love (negative)"

desc_PA_perc_love_neg <- lme(
  fixed = love ~ PA_sim_perc,
  random = ~ 1 | dyad,
  data = sc2_neg,
  method = "REML",
  na.action = na.exclude
)

model_output(desc_PA_perc_love_neg)
```

Perceived PA similarity $\beta_1 = 0.181$, 95% CI $[-0.096, 0.457]$, $p = .198$

Statistically zero. Perceived PA similarity does not predict love after the negative interaction when entered as the sole predictor. This is consistent with the Step 1b results, where perceived PA similarity was also non-significant alongside actual similarity ($-0.081$, $p = .670$). The coefficient here is positive and somewhat larger than in Step 1b, because without actual similarity in the model, perceived similarity captures some of the shared variance between the two indices. But the effect remains non-significant.

```{r desc_PA_perc_love_pos}
#| output-fold: true
#| output-summary: "Descriptive: perceived PA → Love (positive)"

desc_PA_perc_love_pos <- lme(
  fixed = love ~ PA_sim_perc,
  random = ~ 1 | dyad,
  data = sc2_pos,
  method = "REML",
  na.action = na.exclude
)

model_output(desc_PA_perc_love_pos)
```

Perceived PA similarity $\beta_1 = 0.292$, 95% CI $[0.016, 0.567]$, $p = .038$

Significant. Perceived PA similarity predicts love after the positive interaction when entered as the sole predictor. The coefficient is larger and more precisely estimated than in the negative interaction ($0.181$, $p = .198$), and crosses the significance threshold that the negative interaction model did not reach.

Descriptively, the context comparison for perceived PA similarity mirrors the pattern for actual PA similarity (love: $0.379$ negative, $0.459$ positive): the slope is somewhat larger in the positive interaction. However, the perceived similarity slopes are roughly half the magnitude of the actual similarity slopes, and only the positive interaction reaches significance. Whether this descriptive difference between contexts is statistically reliable will be determined by the formal test below.

```{r desc_PA_perc_close_neg}
#| output-fold: true
#| output-summary: "Descriptive: perceived PA → Closeness (negative)"

desc_PA_perc_close_neg <- lme(
  fixed = close ~ PA_sim_perc,
  random = ~ 1 | dyad,
  data = sc2_neg,
  method = "REML",
  na.action = na.exclude
)

model_output(desc_PA_perc_close_neg)
```

Perceived PA similarity $\beta_1 = 0.195$, 95% CI $[-0.079, 0.469]$, $p = .162$

Statistically zero. Perceived PA similarity does not predict closeness after the negative interaction when entered as the sole predictor. The coefficient is nearly identical to the love model in the same context ($0.181$, $p = .198$), confirming that perceived PA similarity is uninformative for both outcomes in the negative interaction.

```{r desc_PA_perc_close_pos}
#| output-fold: true
#| output-summary: "Descriptive: perceived PA → Closeness (positive)"

desc_PA_perc_close_pos <- lme(
  fixed = close ~ PA_sim_perc,
  random = ~ 1 | dyad,
  data = sc2_pos,
  method = "REML",
  na.action = na.exclude
)

model_output(desc_PA_perc_close_pos)
```

Perceived PA similarity $\beta_1 = 0.334$, 95% CI $[0.081, 0.586]$, $p = .010$

Significant, and more precisely estimated than the love model in the same context ($0.292$, $p = .038$). The context comparison mirrors the pattern across all PA models: the slope is larger and significant in the positive interaction ($0.334$) but smaller and non-significant in the negative interaction ($0.195$, $p = .162$). As with love, the perceived similarity slopes are roughly half the magnitude of the actual similarity slopes (closeness: $0.432$ negative, $0.376$ positive), but the context pattern differs: for actual similarity the slopes were similar across contexts, while for perceived similarity only the positive interaction reaches significance. This suggests that perceived PA similarity may be more informative after a positive interaction than after a disagreement, though the formal test will determine whether this descriptive difference is reliable.

The descriptive comparison for perceived PA similarity can now be summarized:

+---------------+-----------------------------+-----------------------------+
| Outcome       | Negative Interaction        | Positive Interaction        |
+===============+=============================+=============================+
| Love          | $\beta = 0.181$, $p = .198$ | $\beta = 0.292$, $p = .038$ |
+---------------+-----------------------------+-----------------------------+
| Closeness     | $\beta = 0.195$, $p = .162$ | $\beta = 0.334$, $p = .010$ |
+---------------+-----------------------------+-----------------------------+

In both outcomes, the perceived PA similarity slope is non-significant in the negative interaction and significant in the positive interaction. The magnitude is consistently larger in the positive context. This pattern is opposite to H3.3's prediction that PA similarity would be more beneficial in the negative interaction.

### NA Similarity

#### Actual NA Similarity

The Step 1a models (`h12_NA_love_neg`, `h12_NA_love_pos`, `h12_NA_close_neg`, `h12_NA_close_pos`) provide the stratified estimates:

+----------------+------------------------------+-----------------------------+
| Outcome        | Negative Interaction         | Positive Interaction        |
+================+==============================+=============================+
| Love           | $\beta = -0.141$, $p = .504$ | $\beta = 0.352$, $p = .009$ |
+----------------+------------------------------+-----------------------------+
| Closeness      | $\beta = -0.157$, $p = .497$ | $\beta = 0.383$, $p = .002$ |
+----------------+------------------------------+-----------------------------+

The context difference is stark: null effects in the negative interaction but significant positive effects in the positive interaction, for both outcomes. The formal test will determine whether this context difference is statistically reliable.

#### Perceived NA Similarity

```{r desc_NA_perc_love_neg}
#| output-fold: true
#| output-summary: "Descriptive: perceived NA → Love (negative)"

desc_NA_perc_love_neg <- lme(
  fixed = love ~ NA_sim_perc,
  random = ~ 1 | dyad,
  data = sc2_neg,
  method = "REML",
  na.action = na.exclude
)

model_output(desc_NA_perc_love_neg)
```

Perceived NA similarity $\beta_1 = 0.005$, 95% CI $[-0.332, 0.343]$, $p = .975$

Statistically zero and essentially exactly zero. Perceived NA similarity does not predict love after the negative interaction. This mirrors the actual NA similarity result in the same context ($-0.141$, $p = .504$): neither form of NA similarity is informative for love after the disagreement.

```{r desc_NA_perc_love_pos}
#| output-fold: true
#| output-summary: "Descriptive: perceived NA → Love (positive)"

desc_NA_perc_love_pos <- lme(
  fixed = love ~ NA_sim_perc,
  random = ~ 1 | dyad,
  data = sc2_pos,
  method = "REML",
  na.action = na.exclude
)

model_output(desc_NA_perc_love_pos)
```

Perceived NA similarity $\beta_1 = 0.201$, 95% CI $[-0.012, 0.413]$, $p = .064$

Just below conventional significance. The coefficient is positive, consistent with the actual NA similarity result in the same context ($0.352$, $p = .009$), though roughly half the magnitude. The context comparison is stark: essentially zero in the negative interaction ($0.005$, $p = .975$) versus marginally non-significant in the positive interaction ($0.201$, $p = .064$). As with actual NA similarity, the descriptive pattern suggests that shared NA during a positive interaction may carry a positive relational signal, though for perceived similarity the evidence does not reach significance even in the positive context.

```{r desc_NA_perc_close_neg}
#| output-fold: true
#| output-summary: "Descriptive: perceived NA → Closeness (negative)"

desc_NA_perc_close_neg <- lme(
  fixed = close ~ NA_sim_perc,
  random = ~ 1 | dyad,
  data = sc2_neg,
  method = "REML",
  na.action = na.exclude
)

model_output(desc_NA_perc_close_neg)
```

Perceived NA similarity $\beta_1 = -0.040$, 95% CI $[-0.370, 0.290]$, $p = .810$

Statistically zero. Perceived NA similarity does not predict closeness after the negative interaction. As with love in the same context ($0.005$, $p = .975$), perceived NA similarity is uninformative for both outcomes in the negative interaction.

```{r desc_NA_perc_close_pos}
#| output-fold: true
#| output-summary: "Descriptive: perceived NA → Closeness (positive)"

desc_NA_perc_close_pos <- lme(
  fixed = close ~ NA_sim_perc,
  random = ~ 1 | dyad,
  data = sc2_pos,
  method = "REML",
  na.action = na.exclude
)

model_output(desc_NA_perc_close_pos)
```

Perceived NA similarity $\beta_1 = 0.109$, 95% CI $[-0.087, 0.306]$, $p = .273$

Statistically zero. Perceived NA similarity does not predict closeness after the positive interaction when entered as the sole predictor. The coefficient is positive, consistent with the direction of the actual NA similarity effect in the same context ($0.383$, $p = .002$), but much smaller and non-significant.

The descriptive comparison for perceived NA similarity can now be summarized:

+---------------+------------------------------+-----------------------------+
| Outcome       | Negative Interaction         | Positive Interaction        |
+===============+==============================+=============================+
| Love          | $\beta = 0.005$, $p = .975$  | $\beta = 0.201$, $p = .064$ |
+---------------+------------------------------+-----------------------------+
| Closeness     | $\beta = -0.040$, $p = .810$ | $\beta = 0.109$, $p = .273$ |
+---------------+------------------------------+-----------------------------+

All four coefficients are non-significant. The context pattern is nonetheless consistent with the actual NA similarity results: slopes are essentially zero in the negative interaction and positive (though non-significant) in the positive interaction. The contrast between contexts is less pronounced than for actual similarity, where the positive interaction slopes were significant for both outcomes. Perceived NA similarity does not reach significance in any context for any outcome when entered as the sole predictor, suggesting that the actual affective overlap rather than its perception is what carries the context-dependent signal observed in Step 1a.

### Summary of descriptive comparison

```{r step3b_descriptive_table}
#| tbl-cap: "Descriptive comparison: Similarity–evaluation coefficients estimated separately within each interaction context"

# --- Build rows ---
rows <- list(
  list("<b>Love</b>", "<b>PA Similarity</b>", "<b>Actual</b>",
       fmt_cell(h11_PA_love_neg, "PA_sim_act"),
       fmt_cell(h11_PA_love_pos, "PA_sim_act")),
  list("<b>Love</b>", "<b>PA Similarity</b>", "<b>Perceived</b>",
       fmt_cell(desc_PA_perc_love_neg, "PA_sim_perc"),
       fmt_cell(desc_PA_perc_love_pos, "PA_sim_perc")),
  list("<b>Love</b>", "<b>NA Similarity</b>", "<b>Actual</b>",
       fmt_cell(h12_NA_love_neg, "NA_sim_act"),
       fmt_cell(h12_NA_love_pos, "NA_sim_act")),
  list("<b>Love</b>", "<b>NA Similarity</b>", "<b>Perceived</b>",
       fmt_cell(desc_NA_perc_love_neg, "NA_sim_perc"),
       fmt_cell(desc_NA_perc_love_pos, "NA_sim_perc")),
  list("<b>Closeness</b>", "<b>PA Similarity</b>", "<b>Actual</b>",
       fmt_cell(h11_PA_close_neg, "PA_sim_act"),
       fmt_cell(h11_PA_close_pos, "PA_sim_act")),
  list("<b>Closeness</b>", "<b>PA Similarity</b>", "<b>Perceived</b>",
       fmt_cell(desc_PA_perc_close_neg, "PA_sim_perc"),
       fmt_cell(desc_PA_perc_close_pos, "PA_sim_perc")),
  list("<b>Closeness</b>", "<b>NA Similarity</b>", "<b>Actual</b>",
       fmt_cell(h12_NA_close_neg, "NA_sim_act"),
       fmt_cell(h12_NA_close_pos, "NA_sim_act")),
  list("<b>Closeness</b>", "<b>NA Similarity</b>", "<b>Perceived</b>",
       fmt_cell(desc_NA_perc_close_neg, "NA_sim_perc"),
       fmt_cell(desc_NA_perc_close_pos, "NA_sim_perc"))
)

tbl_desc <- do.call(rbind, lapply(rows, function(r) {
  data.frame(
    Outcome    = r[[1]],
    Affect     = r[[2]],
    Similarity = r[[3]],
    Positive   = r[[5]],
    Negative   = r[[4]],
    check.names = FALSE,
    stringsAsFactors = FALSE
  )
}))

note_html <- paste0(
  "<em>Note.</em> ",
  "Coefficients with * p &lt; .05, ** p &lt; .01, ",
  "*** p &lt; .001. Significant coefficients (p &lt; .05) in ",
  "<strong>bold</strong>. Actual similarity coefficients are from Step 1a ",
  "models. Perceived similarity coefficients are from analogous bivariate ",
  "models substituting perceived for actual similarity. All models include ",
  "a dyad-level random intercept and are estimated separately within each ",
  "interaction context."
)

tab <- kable(tbl_desc,
  format    = "html",
  col.names = c("Outcome", "Affect", "Similarity",
                "Positive", "Negative"),
  align     = c("l", "l", "l", "c", "c"),
  booktabs  = TRUE,
  escape    = FALSE
) %>%
  kable_styling(
    bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
    full_width = FALSE,
    position   = "left",
    font_size  = 12
  ) %>%
  add_header_above(c(" " = 3,
                      "Interaction Valence" = 2)) %>%
  row_spec(3, extra_css = "border-top: 1px solid #aaa !important;") %>%
  row_spec(5, extra_css = "border-top: 2px solid #bbb !important;") %>%
  row_spec(7, extra_css = "border-top: 1px solid #aaa !important;") %>%
  collapse_rows(columns = 1:2, valign = "middle")

tab_out <- paste0(
  '<style>
    table.table > thead > tr:last-child > th {
      border-bottom: 2px solid #bbb !important;
    }
  </style>',
  sub(
    "</table>",
    paste0(
      "<tfoot><tr><td colspan=\"5\" ",
      "style=\"border-top: 0; padding: 8px 0 0 0; font-size: 11px; ",
      "line-height: 1.35; text-align: left;\">",
      note_html,
      "</td></tr></tfoot></table>"
    ),
    as.character(tab),
    fixed = TRUE
  )
)

attributes(tab_out) <- attributes(tab)
tab_out
```

## Formal Test: Interaction Valence Moderation

We now test whether the similarity–evaluation association differs between contexts using pooled models with a context dummy. A dummy variable $C_{pdc} = c$ ($c = 0$ negative, $c = 1$ positive) is included in pooled models. Since these models contain two observations per person, both dyad-level and person-level random intercepts are included.

For actual similarity:

$$
O_{pdc} = b_{0,pd} + \beta_1 \bar{S}^{act}_{pdc} + \beta_2 C_{pdc} + \beta_3 (\bar{S}^{act}_{pdc} \times C_{pdc}) + \varepsilon_{pdc}
$$

$$
b_{0,pd} = \beta_0 + v_{0,d} + u_{0,pd}
$$

For perceived similarity: analogous model with $\bar{S}^{perc}_{pdc}$.

$\beta_1$ is the similarity–evaluation slope for the negative interaction ($C_{pdc} = 0$), and $\beta_1 + \beta_3$ is the slope for the positive interaction.

For PA (H3.3): expected $\beta_3 < 0$ (PA similarity more beneficial in negative interaction). For NA (H3.4): expected $\beta_3 < 0$ (NA similarity more harmful in positive interaction).

### H3.3 – PA × Context

#### Actual PA Similarity

##### Love

```{r h33_PA_act_love}
#| output-fold: true
#| output-summary: "H3.3 actual PA × context → Love"

h33_PA_act_love <- lme(
  fixed = love ~ PA_sim_act + context + PA_sim_act:context,
  random = ~ 1 | dyad/person,
  data = sc2,
  method = "REML",
  na.action = na.exclude
)

model_output(h33_PA_act_love)
```

**Intercept** $\beta_0 = 84.91$, 95% CI $[78.39, 91.44]$, $p < .001$

Expected post-interaction love at zero actual PA similarity in the negative interaction (context = 0).

**Actual PA similarity** $\beta_1 = 0.226$, 95% CI $[0.011, 0.441]$, $p = .040$

The similarity slope for the negative interaction. Significant, indicating that actual PA similarity predicts love after the disagreement in the pooled model. The coefficient is somewhat attenuated relative to the stratified Step 1a estimate ($0.379$, $p = .020$), likely because the pooled model partitions variance differently with the nested random intercept structure.

**Context** $\beta_2 = 0.839$, 95% CI $[-5.729, 7.408]$, $p = .801$

Statistically zero. At zero actual PA similarity, there is no difference in love between the negative and positive interaction contexts.

**Interaction** $\beta_3 = 0.184$, 95% CI $[-0.184, 0.552]$, $p = .325$

Statistically zero. The actual PA similarity slope does not differ between the negative and positive interaction contexts. H3.3 predicted $\beta_3 < 0$ (PA similarity more beneficial in the negative interaction), but the point estimate is positive (if anything, the slope is slightly larger in the positive interaction), and the CI comfortably includes zero. The positive interaction slope is $\beta_1 + \beta_3 = 0.226 + 0.184 = 0.410$, comparable to the stratified estimate ($0.459$).

**Random effects**

Dyad intercept $SD = 9.50$; person intercept $SD = 11.66$; residual $SD = 11.15$. The person-level variance ($136.01$) is larger than the dyad-level variance ($90.30$), indicating substantial within-dyad differences between partners in post-interaction love, consistent with the Step 1a findings.

**The formal test does not support H3.3 for actual PA similarity predicting love.** The similarity effect does not differ between interaction contexts.

```{r h33_PA_act_love_slopes}
#| output-fold: true
#| output-summary: "Simple slopes — actual PA × context → Love"

# Simple slopes at negative (context = 0) and positive (context = 1) interaction
slopes_h33_act_love <- emtrends(
  h33_PA_act_love,
  specs = ~ context,
  var   = "PA_sim_act",
  at    = list(context = c(0, 1))
)

slopes_h33_act_love_df <- as.data.frame(summary(slopes_h33_act_love, infer = TRUE))

tbl_h33_act_love <- data.frame(
  `Interaction context` = c("Negative (0)", "Positive (1)"),
  `Simple slope`        = sprintf("%.4f", slopes_h33_act_love_df$PA_sim_act.trend),
  `SE`                  = sprintf("%.4f", slopes_h33_act_love_df$SE),
  `95% CI`              = sprintf("[%.4f, %.4f]", slopes_h33_act_love_df$lower.CL, slopes_h33_act_love_df$upper.CL),
  `t`                   = sprintf("%.3f", slopes_h33_act_love_df$t.ratio),
  `p`                   = ifelse(slopes_h33_act_love_df$p.value < .001, "< .001",
                                 sprintf("%.3f", slopes_h33_act_love_df$p.value)),
  check.names = FALSE
)

kable(tbl_h33_act_love,
  align    = c("l", "c", "c", "c", "c", "c"),
  booktabs = TRUE,
  caption  = "Simple slopes of actual PA similarity on love in negative and positive interactions (H3.3)"
) %>%
  kable_styling(
    bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"),
    full_width = FALSE,
    position   = "left"
  )
```

The simple slopes of actual PA similarity on love are significant in both contexts: $0.226$ in the negative interaction ($p = .040$) and $0.410$ in the positive interaction ($p = .001$). Both are positive and significant, confirming that actual PA similarity predicts love regardless of context. The positive interaction slope is nearly twice as large, but the non-significant interaction term ($\beta_3 = 0.184$, $p = .325$) indicates that this difference is not statistically reliable. Descriptively, the pattern is opposite to H3.3's prediction: if anything, PA similarity is more beneficial in the positive interaction, not the negative one.

##### Closeness

```{r h33_PA_act_close}
#| output-fold: true
#| output-summary: "H3.3 actual PA × context → Closeness"

h33_PA_act_close <- lme(
  fixed = close ~ PA_sim_act + context + PA_sim_act:context,
  random = ~ 1 | dyad/person,
  data = sc2,
  method = "REML",
  na.action = na.exclude
)

model_output(h33_PA_act_close)
```

**Intercept** $\beta_0 = 88.80$, 95% CI $[82.49, 95.12]$, $p < .001$

Expected post-interaction closeness at zero actual PA similarity in the negative interaction.

**Actual PA similarity** $\beta_1 = 0.311$, 95% CI $[0.106, 0.517]$, $p = .003$

The similarity slope for the negative interaction. Significant and comparable to the stratified estimate ($0.432$, $p = .015$), with some attenuation in the pooled model.

**Context** $\beta_2 = -2.102$, 95% CI $[-8.387, 4.184]$, $p = .510$

Statistically zero. At zero actual PA similarity, there is no difference in closeness between contexts.

**Interaction** $\beta_3 = -0.063$, 95% CI $[-0.417, 0.291]$, $p = .725$

Statistically zero. The actual PA similarity slope does not differ between contexts. H3.3 predicted $\beta_3 < 0$, and the point estimate is indeed slightly negative, but far from significance. The positive interaction slope is $\beta_1 + \beta_3 = 0.311 - 0.063 = 0.248$, somewhat smaller than the negative interaction slope, but the difference is not reliable. This contrasts with love, where the descriptive pattern went in the opposite direction (larger slope in the positive interaction).

**Random effects**

Dyad intercept $SD = 10.94$; person intercept $SD = 10.05$; residual $SD = 10.52$. The variance components are more evenly distributed across levels than in the love model, with dyad-level variance ($119.67$) slightly exceeding person-level ($101.00$) and residual ($110.62$) variance.

**The formal test does not support H3.3 for actual PA similarity predicting closeness.** The similarity effect does not differ between interaction contexts. Across both outcomes, H3.3 is not supported for actual PA similarity: the interaction terms are non-significant and inconsistent in direction (positive for love, slightly negative for closeness).

```{r h33_PA_act_close_slopes}
#| output-fold: true
#| output-summary: "Simple slopes — actual PA × context → Closeness"

slopes_h33_act_close <- emtrends(
  h33_PA_act_close,
  specs = ~ context,
  var   = "PA_sim_act",
  at    = list(context = c(0, 1))
)

slopes_h33_act_close_df <- as.data.frame(summary(slopes_h33_act_close, infer = TRUE))

tbl_h33_act_close <- data.frame(
  `Interaction context` = c("Negative (0)", "Positive (1)"),
  `Simple slope`        = sprintf("%.4f", slopes_h33_act_close_df$PA_sim_act.trend),
  `SE`                  = sprintf("%.4f", slopes_h33_act_close_df$SE),
  `95% CI`              = sprintf("[%.4f, %.4f]", slopes_h33_act_close_df$lower.CL, slopes_h33_act_close_df$upper.CL),
  `t`                   = sprintf("%.3f", slopes_h33_act_close_df$t.ratio),
  `p`                   = ifelse(slopes_h33_act_close_df$p.value < .001, "< .001",
                                 sprintf("%.3f", slopes_h33_act_close_df$p.value)),
  check.names = FALSE
)

kable(tbl_h33_act_close,
  align    = c("l", "c", "c", "c", "c", "c"),
  booktabs = TRUE,
  caption  = "Simple slopes of actual PA similarity on closeness in negative and positive interactions (H3.3)"
) %>%
  kable_styling(
    bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"),
    full_width = FALSE,
    position   = "left"
  )
```

The simple slopes of actual PA similarity on closeness are significant in both contexts: $0.311$ in the negative interaction ($p = .003$) and $0.248$ in the positive interaction ($p = .042$). As with love, PA similarity predicts the outcome in both contexts. The negative interaction slope is slightly larger, descriptively consistent with H3.3's prediction, but the non-significant interaction term ($\beta_3 = -0.063$, $p = .725$) indicates this difference is not reliable. The slopes are close in magnitude and both significant, suggesting that actual PA similarity operates similarly across contexts for closeness.

#### Perceived PA Similarity

##### Love

```{r h33_PA_perc_love}
#| output-fold: true
#| output-summary: "H3.3 perceived PA × context → Love"

h33_PA_perc_love <- lme(
  fixed = love ~ PA_sim_perc + context + PA_sim_perc:context,
  random = ~ 1 | dyad/person,
  data = sc2,
  method = "REML",
  na.action = na.exclude
)

model_output(h33_PA_perc_love)
```

**Intercept** $\beta_0 = 81.76$, 95% CI $[76.66, 86.87]$, $p < .001$

Expected post-interaction love at zero perceived PA similarity in the negative interaction.

**Perceived PA similarity** $\beta_1 = 0.140$, 95% CI $[-0.057, 0.337]$, $p = .162$

Statistically zero. The perceived PA similarity slope for the negative interaction is non-significant, consistent with the stratified descriptive estimate ($0.181$, $p = .198$).

**Context** $\beta_2 = 1.720$, 95% CI $[-2.921, 6.361]$, $p = .466$

Statistically zero.

**Interaction** $\beta_3 = 0.236$, 95% CI $[-0.117, 0.589]$, $p = .189$

Statistically zero. The perceived PA similarity slope does not differ between contexts. H3.3 predicted $\beta_3 < 0$, but the point estimate is positive. The positive interaction slope is $\beta_1 + \beta_3 = 0.140 + 0.236 = 0.376$, larger than the negative interaction slope, descriptively opposite to H3.3. This is consistent with the descriptive comparison, where perceived PA similarity was significant only in the positive interaction ($0.292$, $p = .038$) and non-significant in the negative interaction ($0.181$, $p = .198$).

**Random effects**

Dyad intercept $SD = 9.54$; person intercept $SD = 11.83$; residual $SD = 11.16$. Comparable to the actual PA similarity model.

**The formal test does not support H3.3 for perceived PA similarity predicting love.** The direction of the (non-significant) context difference is opposite to the hypothesis.

```{r h33_PA_perc_love_slopes}
#| output-fold: true
#| output-summary: "Simple slopes — perceived PA × context → Love"

slopes_h33_perc_love <- emtrends(
  h33_PA_perc_love,
  specs = ~ context,
  var   = "PA_sim_perc",
  at    = list(context = c(0, 1))
)

slopes_h33_perc_love_df <- as.data.frame(summary(slopes_h33_perc_love, infer = TRUE))

tbl_h33_perc_love <- data.frame(
  `Interaction context` = c("Negative (0)", "Positive (1)"),
  `Simple slope`        = sprintf("%.4f", slopes_h33_perc_love_df$PA_sim_perc.trend),
  `SE`                  = sprintf("%.4f", slopes_h33_perc_love_df$SE),
  `95% CI`              = sprintf("[%.4f, %.4f]", slopes_h33_perc_love_df$lower.CL, slopes_h33_perc_love_df$upper.CL),
  `t`                   = sprintf("%.3f", slopes_h33_perc_love_df$t.ratio),
  `p`                   = ifelse(slopes_h33_perc_love_df$p.value < .001, "< .001",
                                 sprintf("%.3f", slopes_h33_perc_love_df$p.value)),
  check.names = FALSE
)

kable(tbl_h33_perc_love,
  align    = c("l", "c", "c", "c", "c", "c"),
  booktabs = TRUE,
  caption  = "Simple slopes of perceived PA similarity on love in negative and positive interactions (H3.3)"
) %>%
  kable_styling(
    bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"),
    full_width = FALSE,
    position   = "left"
  )
```

The simple slopes of perceived PA similarity on love are non-significant in the negative interaction ($0.140$, $p = .162$) and significant in the positive interaction ($0.376$, $p = .002$). The positive interaction slope is nearly three times the magnitude of the negative interaction slope. Despite this descriptive difference, the interaction term is non-significant ($\beta_3 = 0.236$, $p = .189$), so the context difference cannot be treated as reliable. The pattern is consistent with the descriptive comparison and with the broader finding that perceived PA similarity tends to be more informative in the positive interaction, but the formal test does not support H3.3.

##### Closeness

```{r h33_PA_perc_close}
#| output-fold: true
#| output-summary: "H3.3 perceived PA × context → Closeness"

h33_PA_perc_close <- lme(
  fixed = close ~ PA_sim_perc + context + PA_sim_perc:context,
  random = ~ 1 | dyad/person,
  data = sc2,
  method = "REML",
  na.action = na.exclude
)

model_output(h33_PA_perc_close)
```

**Intercept** $\beta_0 = 86.38$, 95% CI $[81.46, 91.31]$, $p < .001$

Expected post-interaction closeness at zero perceived PA similarity in the negative interaction.

**Perceived PA similarity** $\beta_1 = 0.284$, 95% CI $[0.099, 0.470]$, $p = .003$

Significant. The perceived PA similarity slope for the negative interaction is significant in the pooled model, despite being non-significant in the stratified estimate ($0.195$, $p = .162$). The pooled model gains power from estimating variance components across both contexts simultaneously, which can sharpen individual slope estimates.

**Context** $\beta_2 = -1.030$, 95% CI $[-5.386, 3.326]$, $p = .642$

Statistically zero.

**Interaction** $\beta_3 = -0.075$, 95% CI $[-0.408, 0.258]$, $p = .657$

Statistically zero. The perceived PA similarity slope does not differ between contexts. The positive interaction slope is $\beta_1 + \beta_3 = 0.284 - 0.075 = 0.209$, slightly smaller than the negative interaction slope. Unlike the love model, where the descriptive pattern favored the positive interaction, here closeness shows a slight (non-significant) advantage for the negative interaction. Neither direction is reliable.

**Random effects**

Dyad intercept $SD = 10.92$; person intercept $SD = 10.32$; residual $SD = 10.42$. Variance is evenly distributed across the three levels.

**The formal test does not support H3.3 for perceived PA similarity predicting closeness.** Across all four H3.3 models (actual/perceived $\times$ love/closeness), no interaction term reaches significance. H3.3 is not supported in SC2.

```{r h33_PA_perc_close_slopes}
#| output-fold: true
#| output-summary: "Simple slopes — perceived PA × context → Closeness"

slopes_h33_perc_close <- emtrends(
  h33_PA_perc_close,
  specs = ~ context,
  var   = "PA_sim_perc",
  at    = list(context = c(0, 1))
)

slopes_h33_perc_close_df <- as.data.frame(summary(slopes_h33_perc_close, infer = TRUE))

tbl_h33_perc_close <- data.frame(
  `Interaction context` = c("Negative (0)", "Positive (1)"),
  `Simple slope`        = sprintf("%.4f", slopes_h33_perc_close_df$PA_sim_perc.trend),
  `SE`                  = sprintf("%.4f", slopes_h33_perc_close_df$SE),
  `95% CI`              = sprintf("[%.4f, %.4f]", slopes_h33_perc_close_df$lower.CL, slopes_h33_perc_close_df$upper.CL),
  `t`                   = sprintf("%.3f", slopes_h33_perc_close_df$t.ratio),
  `p`                   = ifelse(slopes_h33_perc_close_df$p.value < .001, "< .001",
                                 sprintf("%.3f", slopes_h33_perc_close_df$p.value)),
  check.names = FALSE
)

kable(tbl_h33_perc_close,
  align    = c("l", "c", "c", "c", "c", "c"),
  booktabs = TRUE,
  caption  = "Simple slopes of perceived PA similarity on closeness in negative and positive interactions (H3.3)"
) %>%
  kable_styling(
    bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"),
    full_width = FALSE,
    position   = "left"
  )
```

The simple slopes of perceived PA similarity on closeness are significant in the negative interaction ($0.284$, $p = .003$) and marginally non-significant in the positive interaction ($0.209$, $p = .069$). The negative interaction slope being significant here, despite the stratified estimate being non-significant ($0.195$, $p = .162$), reflects the power gain from the pooled model. The slopes are of comparable magnitude, and the non-significant interaction term ($\beta_3 = -0.075$, $p = .657$) confirms that the context difference is not reliable. This completes the H3.3 series: across all four models and all eight simple slopes, PA similarity (both actual and perceived) consistently predicts post-interaction evaluations in both contexts, with no reliable evidence that the effect differs between the negative and positive interaction.

### H3.4 – NA × Context

#### Actual NA Similarity

##### Love

```{r h34_NA_act_love}
#| output-fold: true
#| output-summary: "H3.4 actual NA × context → Love"

h34_NA_act_love <- lme(
  fixed = love ~ NA_sim_act + context + NA_sim_act:context,
  random = ~ 1 | dyad/person,
  data = sc2,
  method = "REML",
  na.action = na.exclude
)

model_output(h34_NA_act_love)
```

**Intercept** $\beta_0 = 74.98$, 95% CI $[71.10, 78.87]$, $p < .001$

Expected post-interaction love at zero actual NA similarity in the negative interaction.

**Actual NA similarity** $\beta_1 = -0.424$, 95% CI $[-0.693, -0.154]$, $p = .002$

**Significant and negative.** The similarity slope for the negative interaction is significantly negative in the pooled model, despite being non-significant in the stratified Step 1a estimate ($-0.141$, $p = .504$). This is a notable discrepancy: the pooled model, by estimating variance components across both contexts simultaneously, yields a more precise estimate that reaches significance. The direction is consistent with H1.2's original prediction (more shared NA predicts less love), but only emerges in the pooled model.

**Context** $\beta_2 = 11.623$, 95% CI $[6.137, 17.109]$, $p < .001$

**Significant.** At zero actual NA similarity, love is substantially higher after the positive interaction than after the negative interaction. This is the first significant context main effect across all Step 3b models.

**Interaction** $\beta_3 = 0.547$, 95% CI $[0.204, 0.890]$, $p = .002$

**Significant.** The actual NA similarity slope differs between contexts. The positive interaction slope is $\beta_1 + \beta_3 = -0.424 + 0.547 = 0.123$, which is small and positive, contrasting sharply with the negative interaction slope of $-0.424$. The interaction indicates a qualitative reversal: actual NA similarity is negatively associated with love in the negative interaction but positively (though weakly) associated in the positive interaction.

H3.4 predicted $\beta_3 < 0$ (NA similarity more harmful in the positive interaction). The result is the opposite: $\beta_3 > 0$, meaning NA similarity is more harmful in the negative interaction. The hypothesis assumed a negative main effect that would become more negative in the positive context, but the data show that the negative association is specific to the disagreement context and reverses in the positive context.

**Random effects**

Dyad intercept $SD = 10.02$; person intercept $SD = 11.62$; residual $SD = 11.24$.

**This is the first significant context moderation in the Step 3b series, and only the second significant moderation in SC2 overall (after the we-ness crossover in Step 3a).** The result indicates that the relational meaning of shared NA depends on the interaction context: during a disagreement, sharing negative affect predicts less love, while during a positive interaction the same sharing is benign or slightly beneficial. However, the direction is opposite to H3.4's prediction.

```{r h34_NA_act_love_slopes}
#| output-fold: true
#| output-summary: "Simple slopes — actual NA × context → Love"

slopes_h34_act_love <- emtrends(
  h34_NA_act_love,
  specs = ~ context,
  var   = "NA_sim_act",
  at    = list(context = c(0, 1))
)

slopes_h34_act_love_df <- as.data.frame(summary(slopes_h34_act_love, infer = TRUE))

tbl_h34_act_love <- data.frame(
  `Interaction context` = c("Negative (0)", "Positive (1)"),
  `Simple slope`        = sprintf("%.4f", slopes_h34_act_love_df$NA_sim_act.trend),
  `SE`                  = sprintf("%.4f", slopes_h34_act_love_df$SE),
  `95% CI`              = sprintf("[%.4f, %.4f]", slopes_h34_act_love_df$lower.CL, slopes_h34_act_love_df$upper.CL),
  `t`                   = sprintf("%.3f", slopes_h34_act_love_df$t.ratio),
  `p`                   = ifelse(slopes_h34_act_love_df$p.value < .001, "< .001",
                                 sprintf("%.3f", slopes_h34_act_love_df$p.value)),
  check.names = FALSE
)

kable(tbl_h34_act_love,
  align    = c("l", "c", "c", "c", "c", "c"),
  booktabs = TRUE,
  caption  = "Simple slopes of actual NA similarity on love in negative and positive interactions (H3.4)"
) %>%
  kable_styling(
    bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"),
    full_width = FALSE,
    position   = "left"
  )
```

The simple slopes confirm the qualitative reversal identified by the significant interaction. Actual NA similarity is significantly negative in the negative interaction ($-0.424$, $p = .002$) and non-significant in the positive interaction ($0.123$, $p = .214$). Sharing negative affect during a disagreement predicts less love afterward, while during a positive interaction the association is absent.

The negative interaction slope ($-0.424$) is notably larger than the stratified Step 1a estimate ($-0.141$, $p = .504$). The discrepancy arises because the pooled model partitions variance more efficiently: the context main effect and the person-level random intercept absorb variance that was pooled into the residual in the stratified model, yielding a more precise (and here, significant) estimate of the context-specific slope. The positive interaction slope ($0.123$) is smaller and non-significant, consistent with the stratified estimate ($0.352$, $p = .009$) being attenuated in the pooled model. The stratified and pooled models thus tell a coherent story but with different emphasis: the stratified models found a null effect in the negative interaction and a significant positive effect in the positive interaction, while the pooled model finds a significant negative effect in the negative interaction and a non-significant positive effect in the positive interaction. Both agree that the two contexts differ.

##### Closeness

```{r h34_NA_act_close}
#| output-fold: true
#| output-summary: "H3.4 actual NA × context → Closeness"

h34_NA_act_close <- lme(
  fixed = close ~ NA_sim_act + context + NA_sim_act:context,
  random = ~ 1 | dyad/person,
  data = sc2,
  method = "REML",
  na.action = na.exclude
)

model_output(h34_NA_act_close)
```

**Intercept** $\beta_0 = 77.51$, 95% CI $[73.66, 81.37]$, $p < .001$

Expected post-interaction closeness at zero actual NA similarity in the negative interaction.

**Actual NA similarity** $\beta_1 = -0.318$, 95% CI $[-0.577, -0.060]$, $p = .016$

Significant and negative. As with love, the negative interaction slope is significant in the pooled model despite being non-significant in the stratified estimate ($-0.157$, $p = .497$). The same power-gain mechanism applies: the pooled model partitions variance more efficiently, yielding a more precise context-specific estimate.

**Context** $\beta_2 = 7.986$, 95% CI $[2.729, 13.244]$, $p = .003$

Significant. At zero actual NA similarity, closeness is higher after the positive interaction than after the negative interaction, consistent with the love model.

**Interaction** $\beta_3 = 0.313$, 95% CI $[-0.016, 0.643]$, $p = .062$

Marginally non-significant. The positive interaction slope is $\beta_1 + \beta_3 = -0.318 + 0.313 = -0.005$, essentially zero. The pattern is descriptively identical to the love model: a significant negative slope in the negative interaction that reverses (or at least disappears) in the positive interaction. The interaction falls just short of conventional significance ($p = .062$), in contrast to the love model where it was clearly significant ($p = .002$). The direction is again opposite to H3.4's prediction ($\beta_3 < 0$): the negative effect is specific to the disagreement context rather than amplified in the positive context.

**Random effects**

Dyad intercept $SD = 11.46$; person intercept $SD = 9.97$; residual $SD = 10.67$. The dyad-level variance ($131.31$) exceeds person-level ($99.38$) and residual ($113.85$) variance, in contrast to the love model where person-level variance dominated.

**The formal test provides marginal evidence for context moderation of actual NA similarity predicting closeness, in the same direction as the significant love result but not reaching conventional significance.** Across both outcomes, the pattern is consistent: actual NA similarity has a negative association with post-interaction evaluations in the disagreement context that weakens or reverses in the positive context. For love this context difference is significant; for closeness it is suggestive but not reliable at $p < .05$.

```{r h34_NA_act_close_slopes}
#| output-fold: true
#| output-summary: "Simple slopes — actual NA × context → Closeness"

slopes_h34_act_close <- emtrends(
  h34_NA_act_close,
  specs = ~ context,
  var   = "NA_sim_act",
  at    = list(context = c(0, 1))
)

slopes_h34_act_close_df <- as.data.frame(summary(slopes_h34_act_close, infer = TRUE))

tbl_h34_act_close <- data.frame(
  `Interaction context` = c("Negative (0)", "Positive (1)"),
  `Simple slope`        = sprintf("%.4f", slopes_h34_act_close_df$NA_sim_act.trend),
  `SE`                  = sprintf("%.4f", slopes_h34_act_close_df$SE),
  `95% CI`              = sprintf("[%.4f, %.4f]", slopes_h34_act_close_df$lower.CL, slopes_h34_act_close_df$upper.CL),
  `t`                   = sprintf("%.3f", slopes_h34_act_close_df$t.ratio),
  `p`                   = ifelse(slopes_h34_act_close_df$p.value < .001, "< .001",
                                 sprintf("%.3f", slopes_h34_act_close_df$p.value)),
  check.names = FALSE
)

kable(tbl_h34_act_close,
  align    = c("l", "c", "c", "c", "c", "c"),
  booktabs = TRUE,
  caption  = "Simple slopes of actual NA similarity on closeness in negative and positive interactions (H3.4)"
) %>%
  kable_styling(
    bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"),
    full_width = FALSE,
    position   = "left"
  )

```

The simple slopes mirror the love results. Actual NA similarity is significantly negative in the negative interaction ($-0.318$, $p = .016$) and essentially zero in the positive interaction ($-0.005$, $p = .958$). The positive interaction slope is even closer to zero than for love ($0.123$), indicating a complete absence of association rather than a weak positive trend.

The pattern across both outcomes is now clear: actual NA similarity has a context-dependent association with post-interaction evaluations that is significant (love) or marginal (closeness) in the formal test. In the negative interaction, sharing NA predicts lower love and lower closeness; in the positive interaction, the association disappears entirely. The negative interaction slopes ($-0.424$ for love, $-0.318$ for closeness) are larger and more significant than the stratified Step 1a estimates, while the positive interaction slopes ($0.123$ and $-0.005$) are smaller than their stratified counterparts ($0.352$ and $0.383$). This systematic shift reflects the pooled model's different variance partitioning, but the qualitative conclusion is the same: the relational meaning of shared NA depends fundamentally on whether the context is a disagreement or a positive discussion.

#### Perceived NA Similarity

##### Love

```{r h34_NA_perc_love}
#| output-fold: true
#| output-summary: "H3.4 perceived NA × context → Love"

h34_NA_perc_love <- lme(
  fixed = love ~ NA_sim_perc + context + NA_sim_perc:context,
  random = ~ 1 | dyad/person,
  data = sc2,
  method = "REML",
  na.action = na.exclude
)

model_output(h34_NA_perc_love)
```

**Intercept** $\beta_0 = 78.13$, 95% CI $[74.96, 81.30]$, $p < .001$

Expected post-interaction love at zero perceived NA similarity in the negative interaction.

**Perceived NA similarity** $\beta_1 = -0.158$, 95% CI $[-0.394, 0.078]$, $p = .188$

Statistically zero. The perceived NA similarity slope for the negative interaction is non-significant, consistent with the stratified descriptive estimate ($0.005$, $p = .975$). The coefficient is more negative in the pooled model, reflecting the same variance-partitioning shift observed for actual NA similarity.

**Context** $\beta_2 = 7.580$, 95% CI $[3.294, 11.867]$, $p < .001$

Significant. At zero perceived NA similarity, love is higher after the positive interaction, consistent with the actual NA similarity model.

**Interaction** $\beta_3 = 0.251$, 95% CI $[-0.056, 0.558]$, $p = .108$

Statistically zero, though the coefficient is in the same positive direction as the significant actual NA similarity interaction ($0.547$, $p = .002$). The positive interaction slope is $\beta_1 + \beta_3 = -0.158 + 0.251 = 0.093$, small and close to zero. The pattern is descriptively similar to the actual similarity model (negative slope in the negative interaction, near-zero in the positive interaction) but weaker and not reaching significance at any stage.

**Random effects**

Dyad intercept $SD = 9.70$; person intercept $SD = 11.62$; residual $SD = 11.50$.

**The formal test does not support H3.4 for perceived NA similarity predicting love**, though the direction of the interaction is consistent with the significant actual similarity result. The context moderation of NA similarity on love appears to be carried primarily by the actual affective overlap rather than its perception.

```{r h34_NA_perc_love_slopes}
#| output-fold: true
#| output-summary: "Simple slopes — perceived NA × context → Love"

slopes_h34_perc_love <- emtrends(
  h34_NA_perc_love,
  specs = ~ context,
  var   = "NA_sim_perc",
  at    = list(context = c(0, 1))
)

slopes_h34_perc_love_df <- as.data.frame(summary(slopes_h34_perc_love, infer = TRUE))

tbl_h34_perc_love <- data.frame(
  `Interaction context` = c("Negative (0)", "Positive (1)"),
  `Simple slope`        = sprintf("%.4f", slopes_h34_perc_love_df$NA_sim_perc.trend),
  `SE`                  = sprintf("%.4f", slopes_h34_perc_love_df$SE),
  `95% CI`              = sprintf("[%.4f, %.4f]", slopes_h34_perc_love_df$lower.CL, slopes_h34_perc_love_df$upper.CL),
  `t`                   = sprintf("%.3f", slopes_h34_perc_love_df$t.ratio),
  `p`                   = ifelse(slopes_h34_perc_love_df$p.value < .001, "< .001",
                                 sprintf("%.3f", slopes_h34_perc_love_df$p.value)),
  check.names = FALSE
)

kable(tbl_h34_perc_love,
  align    = c("l", "c", "c", "c", "c", "c"),
  booktabs = TRUE,
  caption  = "Simple slopes of perceived NA similarity on love in negative and positive interactions (H3.4)"
) %>%
  kable_styling(
    bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"),
    full_width = FALSE,
    position   = "left"
  )
```

The simple slopes of perceived NA similarity on love are non-significant in both contexts: $-0.158$ in the negative interaction ($p = .188$) and $0.093$ in the positive interaction ($p = .307$). The direction mirrors the actual similarity pattern (negative in the negative interaction, positive in the positive interaction), but neither slope reaches significance. Perceived NA similarity is uninformative for love in both contexts, and the descriptive sign reversal across contexts is not supported by the non-significant interaction term ($\beta_3 = 0.251$, $p = .108$).

##### Closeness

```{r h34_NA_perc_close}
#| output-fold: true
#| output-summary: "H3.4 perceived NA × context → Closeness"

h34_NA_perc_close <- lme(
  fixed = close ~ NA_sim_perc + context + NA_sim_perc:context,
  random = ~ 1 | dyad/person,
  data = sc2,
  method = "REML",
  na.action = na.exclude
)

model_output(h34_NA_perc_close)
```

**Intercept** $\beta_0 = 79.69$, 95% CI $[76.45, 82.92]$, $p < .001$

Expected post-interaction closeness at zero perceived NA similarity in the negative interaction.

**Perceived NA similarity** $\beta_1 = -0.164$, 95% CI $[-0.384, 0.056]$, $p = .144$

Statistically zero. The perceived NA similarity slope for the negative interaction is non-significant, though the coefficient is more negative than the stratified descriptive estimate ($-0.040$, $p = .810$), reflecting the same variance-partitioning shift observed across the pooled NA models.

**Context** $\beta_2 = 3.521$, 95% CI $[-0.482, 7.525]$, $p = .084$

Marginally non-significant. At zero perceived NA similarity, closeness tends to be higher after the positive interaction, but the effect does not reach significance.

**Interaction** $\beta_3 = 0.038$, 95% CI $[-0.250, 0.325]$, $p = .796$

Statistically zero. The perceived NA similarity slope does not differ between contexts. The positive interaction slope is $\beta_1 + \beta_3 = -0.164 + 0.038 = -0.126$, still negative and non-significant. Unlike the actual similarity models, where the slopes reversed sign across contexts, the perceived similarity slopes are negative in both contexts and non-significant in both.

**Random effects**

Dyad intercept $SD = 11.61$; person intercept $SD = 9.98$; residual $SD = 10.66$.

**The formal test does not support H3.4 for perceived NA similarity predicting closeness.** This completes the H3.4 series. Across the four models, only the actual NA similarity model for love shows a significant context moderation ($p = .002$), with the closeness model showing a marginal trend in the same direction ($p = .062$). Perceived NA similarity shows no context moderation for either outcome. The context-dependent reversal of NA similarity effects is carried by the actual affective overlap, not its perception.

```{r h34_NA_perc_close_slopes}
#| output-fold: true
#| output-summary: "Simple slopes — perceived NA × context → Closeness"

slopes_h34_perc_close <- emtrends(
  h34_NA_perc_close,
  specs = ~ context,
  var   = "NA_sim_perc",
  at    = list(context = c(0, 1))
)

slopes_h34_perc_close_df <- as.data.frame(summary(slopes_h34_perc_close, infer = TRUE))

tbl_h34_perc_close <- data.frame(
  `Interaction context` = c("Negative (0)", "Positive (1)"),
  `Simple slope`        = sprintf("%.4f", slopes_h34_perc_close_df$NA_sim_perc.trend),
  `SE`                  = sprintf("%.4f", slopes_h34_perc_close_df$SE),
  `95% CI`              = sprintf("[%.4f, %.4f]", slopes_h34_perc_close_df$lower.CL, slopes_h34_perc_close_df$upper.CL),
  `t`                   = sprintf("%.3f", slopes_h34_perc_close_df$t.ratio),
  `p`                   = ifelse(slopes_h34_perc_close_df$p.value < .001, "< .001",
                                 sprintf("%.3f", slopes_h34_perc_close_df$p.value)),
  check.names = FALSE
)

kable(tbl_h34_perc_close,
  align    = c("l", "c", "c", "c", "c", "c"),
  booktabs = TRUE,
  caption  = "Simple slopes of perceived NA similarity on closeness in negative and positive interactions (H3.4)"
) %>%
  kable_styling(
    bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"),
    full_width = FALSE,
    position   = "left"
  )
```

The simple slopes of perceived NA similarity on closeness are non-significant and nearly identical across contexts: $-0.164$ in the negative interaction ($p = .144$) and $-0.126$ in the positive interaction ($p = .139$). Both are negative and of comparable magnitude, consistent with the near-zero interaction term ($\beta_3 = 0.038$, $p = .796$). Unlike the actual similarity models, where the slopes reversed sign across contexts, perceived NA similarity shows a uniformly negative (though non-significant) trend in both contexts.

This completes the Step 3b formal test series. A summary table of the interaction terms across all eight models:

+------------------------------+--------------------------------+--------------------------------+
|                              | Actual                         | Perceived                      |
+==============================+================================+================================+
| **H3.3 PA** $\times$ Context |                                |                                |
+------------------------------+--------------------------------+--------------------------------+
| Love                         | $\beta_3 = 0.184$, $p = .325$  | $\beta_3 = 0.236$, $p = .189$  |
+------------------------------+--------------------------------+--------------------------------+
| Closeness                    | $\beta_3 = -0.063$, $p = .725$ | $\beta_3 = -0.075$, $p = .657$ |
+------------------------------+--------------------------------+--------------------------------+
| **H3.4 NA** $\times$ Context |                                |                                |
+------------------------------+--------------------------------+--------------------------------+
| Love                         | $\beta_3 = 0.547$, $p = .002$  | $\beta_3 = 0.251$, $p = .108$  |
+------------------------------+--------------------------------+--------------------------------+
| Closeness                    | $\beta_3 = 0.313$, $p = .062$  | $\beta_3 = 0.038$, $p = .796$  |
+------------------------------+--------------------------------+--------------------------------+

H3.3 is not supported: PA similarity effects do not differ between contexts for any outcome or similarity type. H3.4 receives partial support, but in the opposite direction to the hypothesis: actual NA similarity is more negatively associated with evaluations in the negative interaction (not more harmful in the positive interaction as predicted). This context moderation is significant for love ($p = .002$), marginal for closeness ($p = .062$), and absent for perceived similarity.

## Summary for step 3b

```{r step3b_formal_table}
#| tbl-cap: "Summary of Step 3b formal test: Interaction valence moderation of similarity–evaluation associations (H3.3–H3.4)"

# --- Build rows ---
rows <- list(
  list("<b>Closeness</b>", "<b>PA Similarity</b>", "<b>Actual</b>", "<b>Similarity</b>",
       fmt_cell(h33_PA_act_close, "PA_sim_act")),
  list("<b>Closeness</b>", "<b>PA Similarity</b>", "<b>Actual</b>", "<b>Interaction Valence</b>",
       fmt_cell(h33_PA_act_close, "context")),
  list("<b>Closeness</b>", "<b>PA Similarity</b>", "<b>Actual</b>", "<b>Similarity × Interaction Valence</b>",
       fmt_cell(h33_PA_act_close, "PA_sim_act:context")),
  list("<b>Closeness</b>", "<b>PA Similarity</b>", "<b>Perceived</b>", "<b>Similarity</b>",
       fmt_cell(h33_PA_perc_close, "PA_sim_perc")),
  list("<b>Closeness</b>", "<b>PA Similarity</b>", "<b>Perceived</b>", "<b>Interaction Valence</b>",
       fmt_cell(h33_PA_perc_close, "context")),
  list("<b>Closeness</b>", "<b>PA Similarity</b>", "<b>Perceived</b>", "<b>Similarity × Interaction Valence</b>",
       fmt_cell(h33_PA_perc_close, "PA_sim_perc:context")),
  list("<b>Closeness</b>", "<b>NA Similarity</b>", "<b>Actual</b>", "<b>Similarity</b>",
       fmt_cell(h34_NA_act_close, "NA_sim_act")),
  list("<b>Closeness</b>", "<b>NA Similarity</b>", "<b>Actual</b>", "<b>Interaction Valence</b>",
       fmt_cell(h34_NA_act_close, "context")),
  list("<b>Closeness</b>", "<b>NA Similarity</b>", "<b>Actual</b>", "<b>Similarity × Interaction Valence</b>",
       fmt_cell(h34_NA_act_close, "NA_sim_act:context")),
  list("<b>Closeness</b>", "<b>NA Similarity</b>", "<b>Perceived</b>", "<b>Similarity</b>",
       fmt_cell(h34_NA_perc_close, "NA_sim_perc")),
  list("<b>Closeness</b>", "<b>NA Similarity</b>", "<b>Perceived</b>", "<b>Interaction Valence</b>",
       fmt_cell(h34_NA_perc_close, "context")),
  list("<b>Closeness</b>", "<b>NA Similarity</b>", "<b>Perceived</b>", "<b>Similarity × Interaction Valence</b>",
       fmt_cell(h34_NA_perc_close, "NA_sim_perc:context")),
  list("<b>Love</b>", "<b>PA Similarity</b>", "<b>Actual</b>", "<b>Similarity</b>",
       fmt_cell(h33_PA_act_love, "PA_sim_act")),
  list("<b>Love</b>", "<b>PA Similarity</b>", "<b>Actual</b>", "<b>Interaction Valence</b>",
       fmt_cell(h33_PA_act_love, "context")),
  list("<b>Love</b>", "<b>PA Similarity</b>", "<b>Actual</b>", "<b>Similarity × Interaction Valence</b>",
       fmt_cell(h33_PA_act_love, "PA_sim_act:context")),
  list("<b>Love</b>", "<b>PA Similarity</b>", "<b>Perceived</b>", "<b>Similarity</b>",
       fmt_cell(h33_PA_perc_love, "PA_sim_perc")),
  list("<b>Love</b>", "<b>PA Similarity</b>", "<b>Perceived</b>", "<b>Interaction Valence</b>",
       fmt_cell(h33_PA_perc_love, "context")),
  list("<b>Love</b>", "<b>PA Similarity</b>", "<b>Perceived</b>", "<b>Similarity × Interaction Valence</b>",
       fmt_cell(h33_PA_perc_love, "PA_sim_perc:context")),
  list("<b>Love</b>", "<b>NA Similarity</b>", "<b>Actual</b>", "<b>Similarity</b>",
       fmt_cell(h34_NA_act_love, "NA_sim_act")),
  list("<b>Love</b>", "<b>NA Similarity</b>", "<b>Actual</b>", "<b>Interaction Valence</b>",
       fmt_cell(h34_NA_act_love, "context")),
  list("<b>Love</b>", "<b>NA Similarity</b>", "<b>Actual</b>", "<b>Similarity × Interaction Valence</b>",
       fmt_cell(h34_NA_act_love, "NA_sim_act:context")),
  list("<b>Love</b>", "<b>NA Similarity</b>", "<b>Perceived</b>", "<b>Similarity</b>",
       fmt_cell(h34_NA_perc_love, "NA_sim_perc")),
  list("<b>Love</b>", "<b>NA Similarity</b>", "<b>Perceived</b>", "<b>Interaction Valence</b>",
       fmt_cell(h34_NA_perc_love, "context")),
  list("<b>Love</b>", "<b>NA Similarity</b>", "<b>Perceived</b>", "<b>Similarity × Interaction Valence</b>",
       fmt_cell(h34_NA_perc_love, "NA_sim_perc:context"))
)

tbl_3b <- do.call(rbind, lapply(rows, function(r) {
  data.frame(
    Outcome    = r[[1]],
    Affect     = r[[2]],
    Similarity = r[[3]],
    Parameter  = r[[4]],
    Estimate   = r[[5]],
    check.names = FALSE,
    stringsAsFactors = FALSE
  )
}))

note_html <- paste0(
  "<em>Note.</em> ",
  "Coefficients with * p &lt; .05, ** p &lt; .01, ",
  "*** p &lt; .001. Significant coefficients (p &lt; .05) in ",
  "<strong>bold</strong>. Similarity: slope for the negative interaction ",
  "(context = 0). Context: difference in intercept between positive and ",
  "negative interaction. Sim &times; Context: difference in similarity ",
  "slope between contexts (H3.3 for PA, H3.4 for NA; expected negative). ",
  "All models include dyad-level and person-level random intercepts."
)

tab <- kable(tbl_3b,
  format    = "html",
  col.names = c("Outcome", "Affect", "Similarity", "Parameter", "Estimate"),
  align     = c("l", "l", "l", "l", "c"),
  booktabs  = TRUE,
  escape    = FALSE
) %>%
  kable_styling(
    bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
    full_width = FALSE,
    position   = "left",
    font_size  = 12
  ) %>%
  row_spec(4, extra_css = "border-top: 1px solid #aaa !important;") %>%
  row_spec(7, extra_css = "border-top: 1px solid #aaa !important;") %>%
  row_spec(10, extra_css = "border-top: 1px solid #aaa !important;") %>%
  row_spec(13, extra_css = "border-top: 2px solid #bbb !important;") %>%
  row_spec(16, extra_css = "border-top: 1px solid #aaa !important;") %>%
  row_spec(19, extra_css = "border-top: 1px solid #aaa !important;") %>%
  row_spec(22, extra_css = "border-top: 1px solid #aaa !important;") %>%
  collapse_rows(columns = 1:3, valign = "middle")

tab_out <- paste0(
  '<style>
    table.table > thead > tr > th {
      border-bottom: 2px solid #bbb !important;
    }
  </style>',
  sub(
    "</table>",
    paste0(
      "<tfoot><tr><td colspan=\"5\" ",
      "style=\"border-top: 0; padding: 8px 0 0 0; font-size: 11px; ",
      "line-height: 1.35; text-align: left;\">",
      note_html,
      "</td></tr></tfoot></table>"
    ),
    as.character(tab),
    fixed = TRUE
  )
)

attributes(tab_out) <- attributes(tab)
tab_out
```
